mid_tier_book_red_white__royal_blue: Red, White & Royal Blue is an LGBT romance novel by American author Casey McQuiston, originally published on May 14, 2019, by St Martin's Griffin. It centers on the character of Alex Claremont-Diaz, the first son of the United States, and his romantic relationship with Prince Henry George Edward James Fox-Mountchristen-Windsor, a British prince. Upon release, the novel received positive reviews with praise given to the representation of a queer male relationship. The film adaptation was released on August 11, 2023.
mid_tier_book_one_last_stop: One Last Stop is a 2021 LGBTQ romance novel written by American author Casey McQuiston. The novel is about a woman named August Landry, a cynical pseudo detective, who finds love in a woman named Jane Su she meets on a subway, a punk butch lesbian from the 1970s who has been misplaced in time and is trapped on the subway.
mid_tier_book_babel: Babel is a book by Patti Smith, published in 1978, and contains Smith's poems along with her prose, lyrics, pictures and drawings.
mid_tier_book_yellowface: Yellowface is a 2023 satirical novel written by R. F. Kuang. The book was described as a satire of racial diversity in the publishing industry as well as a metafiction about social media, particularly Twitter. Although Yellowface is written as a stand-alone project, Kuang has not ruled out a possible sequel. In late 2024, the screen rights options were sold to Lionsgate Television with Karyn Kusama slated to direct and executive produce a potential limited series based upon this novel with Constance Wu, Justine Suzanne Jones, Ben Smith, and Adam Docksey announced as producers.
mid_tier_book_the_poppy_war: The Poppy War is a 2018 high fantasy novel by American author R. F. Kuang, published by Harper Voyager. A grimdark fantasy, the book's plot draws on politics from mid-20th-century China, with the conflict in the novel inspired by the Second Sino-Japanese War, and an atmosphere evocative of the Song dynasty. A sequel, The Dragon Republic, was released in August 2019, and a third book, The Burning God, was released November 2020. Harper Voyager's editorial director David Pomerico acquired the novel after a heated auction on Kuang's 20th birthday.
mid_tier_book_mistborn_the_final_empire: Mistborn: The Final Empire is a fantasy novel written by American author Brandon Sanderson. It was published on July 17, 2006, by Tor Books and is the first novel in the Mistborn trilogy, followed by The Well of Ascension in 2007 and The Hero of Ages in 2008. The novel is set in the Final Empire, a tyrannical regime led by the seemingly immortal Lord Ruler. The human population is mainly divided into the nobility and the skaa. In the setting, the magic system of Allomancy allows certain people to digest and burn metals to obtain certain magical powers. While most Allomancers are only able to burn one type of metal, which grants them one of the powers, people known as Mistborn can burn any metal. The skaa are heavily oppressed by the nobility and seen as genetically inferior. In The Final Empire, the half-skaa Mistborn Kelsier and his crew recruit a street urchin named Vin and plot to overthrow the Final Empire.
mid_tier_book_stormlight_archive_the_way: The Way of Kings is an epic fantasy novel written by American author Brandon Sanderson and the first book in The Stormlight Archive series. The novel was published on August 31, 2010, by Tor Books. The Way of Kings consists of one prelude, one prologue, 75 chapters, an epilogue, and nine interludes. It was followed by Words of Radiance in 2014, Oathbringer in 2017, Rhythm of War in 2020 and Wind and Truth in 2024. A leatherbound edition was released in 2021. The story rotates between the points of view of Kaladin, Shallan Davar, Dalinar Kholin, Adolin Kholin, Szeth-son-son-Vallano, and several other minor characters, who lead seemingly unconnected lives. In 2011, it won the David Gemmell Legend Award for best novel. The unabridged audiobook is read by narrator team Michael Kramer and Kate Reading.
mid_tier_book_project_hail_mary: Project Hail Mary is a 2021 hard science fiction novel by American writer Andy Weir. It centers on science teacher and former biologist Ryland Grace, who wakes up aboard a spacecraft, afflicted with amnesia. Project Hail Mary received generally positive reviews and was a finalist for the 2022 Hugo Award for Best Novel. The unabridged audiobook was read by Ray Porter and won the 2022 Audie Award for Audiobook of the Year. The book was adapted into a film of the same name starring Ryan Gosling and Sandra Hüller, with Drew Goddard writing (his second Andy Weir adaptation) and Phil Lord and Christopher Miller directing. It was released to theaters on March 20, 2026. As of May 6, 2026, the book has been featured on the New York Times' best-seller list for 47 weeks in total.
mid_tier_book_artemis: Artemis is a 2017 science fiction novel by American writer Andy Weir. It takes place in the late 2080s in Artemis, the first and only city on the Moon. It follows the life of porter and smuggler Jasmine "Jazz" Bashara as she gets caught up in a conspiracy for control of the city. The book received mixed reviews from critics. The audiobook edition (published by Amazon.com) is narrated by Rosario Dawson.
mid_tier_book_the_three_body_problem: The Three-Body Problem (Chinese: 三体; pinyin: Sān tǐ; lit. 'three body') is a 2008 novel by the Chinese hard science fiction author Liu Cixin. It is the first novel in the Remembrance of Earth's Past trilogy. The series portrays a fictional past, present, and future wherein Earth encounters an alien civilization from a nearby system of three Sun-like stars orbiting one another, a representative example of the three-body problem in orbital mechanics. The story was originally serialized in Science Fiction World in 2006 before it was published as a standalone book in 2008. In 2006, it received the Galaxy Award for Chinese science fiction. In 2012, it was described as one of China's most successful full-length novels of the past two decades. The English translation by Ken Liu was published by Tor Books in 2014. That translation was the first novel by an Asian writer to win a Hugo Award for Best Novel; it was also nominated for the Nebula Award for Best Novel. The book has been adapted into other media.
mid_tier_book_the_dark_forest: The Dark Forest (Chinese: 黑暗森林) is a 2008 science fiction novel by Liu Cixin. It is the sequel to the Hugo Award-winning novel The Three-Body Problem in the Remembrance of Earth's Past. The English version, translated by Joel Martinsen, was published in 2015. The novel revolves around humanity's attempts to construct a defence against an impending invasion fleet from an alien planet. It explores the dark forest hypothesis (so-named after the novel), a possible solution to the Fermi paradox, though similar theories have been described as early as 1983.
mid_tier_book_deaths_end: Death's End (Chinese: 死神永生) is a science fiction novel by the Chinese writer Liu Cixin. It is the third novel in the trilogy titled Remembrance of Earth's Past, following the Hugo Award-winning novel The Three-Body Problem and its sequel, The Dark Forest. The original Chinese version was published in 2010. Ken Liu translated the English edition in 2016. It was a finalist for the 2017 Hugo Award for Best Novel and winner of the 2017 Locus Award for Best Science Fiction Novel.
mid_tier_book_all_systems_red: All Systems Red is a 2017 science fiction novella by American author Martha Wells. The first in the Murderbot Diaries series, it was published by Tor.com. The series is about a cyborg designed to protect humans on a research mission. The cyborg narrates the story (hence, "diary") and calls itself "Murderbot". It has developed independence from its original programming by overriding its "governor module" and prefers watching soap operas over its security function. As it spends more time with some caring humans, it starts developing emotions that make it feel uncomfortable.
mid_tier_book_network_effect: Network Effect is a 2020 science fiction novel written by Martha Wells. It is the fifth work in the Murderbot Diaries series and the first full-length novel. Network Effect won the 2021 Hugo Award for Best Novel, the 2020 Nebula Award for Best Novel, and the 2021 Locus Award for Best Science Fiction Novel.
mid_tier_book_gideon_the_ninth: Gideon the Ninth is a 2019 science fantasy novel by the New Zealand writer Tamsyn Muir. It is Muir's debut novel and the first in her The Locked Tomb series, followed by Harrow the Ninth (2020), Nona the Ninth (2022), and the upcoming Alecto the Ninth.
mid_tier_book_harrow_the_ninth: Harrow the Ninth is a 2020 science fantasy novel by the New Zealand writer Tamsyn Muir. It is the second in Muir's The Locked Tomb series, preceded by Gideon the Ninth (2019) and followed by Nona the Ninth (2022) and forthcoming Alecto the Ninth (TBC).
mid_tier_book_nona_the_ninth: Nona the Ninth is a 2022 science fantasy novel by the New Zealand writer Tamsyn Muir. It is the third book in her The Locked Tomb series, after Gideon the Ninth (2019) and Harrow the Ninth (2020), with Alecto the Ninth to follow. The novel won the 2023 Premio Ignotus for Best Foreign Novel. It was a finalist for the 2022 Nebula Award for Best Novel, 2023 Hugo Award for Best Novel and 2023 Locus Award for Best Science Fiction Novel.
mid_tier_book_children_of_time: Children of Time, published on June 4, 2015, is a science fiction novel by author Adrian Tchaikovsky. The novel has two plot strands, one of which follows the evolution of a civilization of genetically modified Portia labiata (arachnoid) on a terraformed exoplanet, guided by an artificial intelligence based on the personality of one of the human terraformers of the planet. The second plot strand follows the journey of an interstellar ark ship containing cryonically preserved humans as they seek a new planetary home following a planetwide environmental collapse on Earth. The novel received positive reviews, and won the 2016 Arthur C. Clarke Award for best science fiction novel. The director of the award program praised the novel as having "universal scale and sense of wonder reminiscent of Clarke himself." The next in the series, Children of Ruin, was published on May 14, 2019. The third book, Children of Memory, was published on November 24, 2022. The fourth and latest book, Children of Strife, was published on March 13, 2026.
mid_tier_book_children_of_ruin: Children of Ruin is a 2019 science fiction novel by British author Adrian Tchaikovsky, the second in his Children of Time series. The novel was well received, winning the 2019 BSFA Award for Best Novel. The book was followed by the third book in the series, Children of Memory, in 2022, and the fourth book, Children of Strife, in 2026.
mid_tier_book_she_who_became_the_sun: She Who Became the Sun is a 2021 historical fantasy novel by Shelley Parker-Chan. Parker-Chan's debut novel, the work is a re-imagining of the rise to power of the Hongwu Emperor in the 14th century. A sequel, He Who Drowned the World, was published in 2023; the two books form The Radiant Emperor Duology. The book won both the Best Novel and Best Newcomer awards at the British Fantasy Awards, and was a finalist for the Lambda Literary Award for Transgender Fiction and the Hugo Award for Best Novel, making Parker-Chan the first Australian to be a finalist for the latter award.
mid_tier_book_the_final_strife: The Final Strife is a novel written by Sudanese-Arab-Ghanaian-British author Saara El-Arifi. It was published in 2021 by Del Rey Books in the United States and HarperVoyager in the United Kingdom. It is the first novel in a trilogy named The Ending Fire.
mid_tier_book_the_city_we_became: The City We Became is a 2020 urban fantasy novel by American writer N. K. Jemisin. It was developed from her short story "The City Born Great,” first published in her collection How Long 'til Black Future Month? It is the first novel in her Great Cities duology and her first novel since completing her Broken Earth series.
mid_tier_book_the_fifth_season: The Fifth Season is a 2015 science fantasy novel by American writer N. K. Jemisin. It was awarded the Hugo Award for Best Novel in 2016. It is the first volume in the Broken Earth series and is followed by The Obelisk Gate and The Stone Sky. The Fifth Season takes place on a planet with a single supercontinent called the Stillness. Every few centuries, its inhabitants endure what they call a "Fifth Season" of catastrophic climate change.
mid_tier_book_the_stone_sky: The Stone Sky is a 2017 science fantasy novel by American writer N. K. Jemisin. It was awarded the Hugo Award for Best Novel, the Nebula Award for Best Novel, and the Locus Award for Best Fantasy Novel in 2018. Reviews of the book upon its release were highly positive. It is the third volume in the Broken Earth series, following The Fifth Season and The Obelisk Gate, both of which also won the Hugo Award.
mid_tier_book_riot_baby: Riot Baby is a science fiction novella written by Nigerian-American author Tochi Onyebuchi published in 2020.
mid_tier_book_trail_of_lightning: Trail of Lightning is a 2018 fantasy novel, the debut novel by Rebecca Roanhorse. After a supernatural disaster destroys most of North America, Navajo monster-slayer Maggie Hoskie must navigate a post-apocalyptic world of monsters and Native American gods. The novel won the 2019 Locus Award for Best First Novel and was a finalist for the Hugo, World Fantasy, and Nebula awards. The novel introduces a number of Navajo-inspired names for Maggie's antagonists. Neizghání is the most important of these, serving as mentor and adversary.

mid_tier_book_a_memory_called_empire: A Memory Called Empire is a 2019 science fiction novel, the debut novel by Arkady Martine. It follows Mahit Dzmare, the ambassador from Lsel Station to the Teixcalaanli Empire, as she investigates the death of her predecessor and the instabilities that underpin that society. The book won the 2020 Compton Crook Award and the 2020 Hugo Award for Best Novel.
mid_tier_book_a_desolation_called_peace: A Desolation Called Peace is a 2021 space opera science fiction novel by Arkady Martine. It is the sequel to A Memory Called Empire, and the second novel in Martine's Teixcalaan series. Like its predecessor, the book won the Hugo Award for Best Novel. It also won the 2022 Locus Award for Best Science Fiction Novel.
mid_tier_book_to_be_taught_if_fortunate: To Be Taught, If Fortunate is a 2019 science fiction novella by Becky Chambers set in the 22nd century featuring speculative astrobiology. It follows four scientists as they explore habitable worlds 15 light years from Earth.
mid_tier_book_the_long_way_to_a_small_an: The Long Way to a Small, Angry Planet is the 2014 debut science fiction novel by Becky Chambers, set in her fictional universe, the Galactic Commons. Chambers originally self-published the novel via a Kickstarter campaign; it was subsequently re-published by Hodder & Stoughton.
mid_tier_book_a_psalm_for_the_wild_built: A Psalm for the Wild-Built is a 2021 solarpunk novella written by American author Becky Chambers, published by Tor Books on July 13, 2021. It is the first book in the Monk & Robot duology, followed by A Prayer for the Crown-Shy, which was released on July 12, 2022. It won the Hugo Award in 2022.
mid_tier_book_the_phoenix_project: Phoenix Project may refer to: Phoenix Project (Pentagon), a part of the Pentagon Renovation Program The Phoenix Project (film), a 2015 film directed by Tyler Graham Pavey The Phoenix Project: Shifting from Oil to Hydrogen, a book by Harry Braun Project Phoenix (SETI) or Phoenix Project, a search for extraterrestrial intelligence in radio signal patterns The Phoenix Project (San Francisco) a nonprofit advocacy group in San Francisco, California The Phoenix Project: A Novel About IT, DevOps, and Helping Your Business Win, a fundamental book that introduced the concept of DevOps by Gene Kim, Kevin Behr, and George Spafford.
mid_tier_book_the_goal: The Goal is a management-oriented novel by Eliyahu M. Goldratt, a business consultant known for his theory of constraints and Jeff Cox, the author of several management-oriented novels. The Goal was originally published in 1984 and has been revised and republished. It describes a case study in operations management, focusing on the theory of constraints and bottlenecks in addition to how to alleviate them. In 2011, Time listed the book as being one of "the 25 most influential business management books".
mid_tier_book_the_five_dysfunctions_of_a: The Five Dysfunctions of a Team is a business book by consultant and speaker Patrick Lencioni first published in 2002. It describes many pitfalls that teams face as they seek to "grow together". This book explores the fundamental causes of organizational politics and team failure. Like most of Lencioni's books, the bulk of it is written as a business fable. The issues it describes were considered especially important to team sports. The book's lessons were applied by several coaches to their teams in the National Football League in the United States.
mid_tier_book_drive: Drive is a 2005 noir novel by American author James Sallis. The book was first published on September 1, 2005, through Poisoned Pen Press. A sequel novel, Driven, was published in 2012.

mid_tier_book_daring_greatly: Daring Greatly is a 2012 self-help book written by Brené Brown. It is a New York Times bestseller and covers topics of vulnerability and shame.
mid_tier_book_atlas_of_the_heart: Atlas of the Heart is a 2021 non-fiction book written by Brené Brown. The book describes human emotions and experiences and the language used to understand them. It is a USA Today bestseller and was developed into a five-episode series for HBO Max. A portion of the series premiered at SXSW on March 11, 2022.
mid_tier_book_the_power_of_habit: The Power of Habit: Why We Do What We Do in Life and Business is a book by Charles Duhigg, a New York Times reporter, published in February 2012 by Random House. mid_tier_book_the_checklist_manifesto: The Checklist Manifesto: How to Get Things Right is a December 2009 non-fiction book by Atul Gawande. It was released on December 22, 2009, through Metropolitan Books and focuses on the use of checklists in relation to several elements of daily and professional life. The book looks at the use of checklists in the business world and the medical profession, with Gawande examining how they can be used for greater efficiency, consistency and safety. Gawande stated he was inspired to write The Checklist Manifesto after reading a story about a young child who survived a fall into a frozen pond and discovering that the physician who saved her relied heavily on checklists. Critical reception for the book has been mostly positive, with Newsday calling it "thoughtfully written". The Seattle Times also gave a positive review.
mid_tier_book_being_mortal: Being Mortal: Medicine and What Matters in the End is a 2014 non-fiction book by American surgeon Atul Gawande. The book addresses end-of-life care, hospice care, and also contains Gawande's reflections and personal stories. He suggests that medical care should focus on well-being rather than survival. Being Mortal has won awards, appeared on lists of best books, and been featured in a documentary.

mid_tier_book_sapiens: Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind (Hebrew: קיצור תולדות האנושות, Qitzur Toldot ha-Enoshut) is a 2011 book by the Israeli military historian Yuval Noah Harari, based on a series of lectures he gave at the Hebrew University of Jerusalem. It was first published in Hebrew in Israel in 2011, and in English in 2014. The book surveys the history of humankind, beginning in the Stone Age and ending in the 21st century. The account relies on a fusion of the natural sciences with the social sciences.
mid_tier_book_homo_deus: Homo Deus: A Brief History of Tomorrow (Hebrew: ההיסטוריה של המחר (Romanised: hahistoria shel hamachar), English: The History of the Tomorrow) is a book written by Israeli author Yuval Noah Harari, professor at the Hebrew University of Jerusalem. The book was first published in Hebrew in 2015 by Dvir publishing; the English-language version was published in September 2016 in the United Kingdom and in February 2017 in the United States. As with its predecessor, Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind, Harari recounts the course of human history while describing events and the individual human experience, along with ethical issues in relation to his historical survey. However, Homo Deus (from Latin "Homo" meaning man or human and "Deus" meaning God) deals more with the abilities acquired by humans (Homo sapiens) throughout their existence, and their evolution as the dominant species in the world. The book describes mankind's current abilities and achievements and attempts to paint an image of the future. Many philosophical issues are discussed, such as humanism, individualism, transhumanism, and mortality.
mid_tier_book_21_lessons_for_the_21st_ce: 21 Lessons for the 21st Century is a book written by Israeli author Yuval Noah Harari and published in August 2018 by Spiegel & Grau in the US and by Jonathan Cape in the UK. It is dedicated to the author's husband, Itzik. The book consists of five parts, each containing four or five essays. The book focuses on present-day issues and societal questions.
mid_tier_book_guns_germs_and_steel: Guns, Germs, and Steel: The Fates of Human Societies (subtitled A Short History of Everybody for the Last 13,000 Years in Britain) is a 1997 transdisciplinary nonfiction book by the American author Jared Diamond. The book attempts to explain why Eurasian and North African civilizations have survived and conquered others, while arguing against the idea that Eurasian hegemony is due to any form of Eurasian intellectual, moral, or inherent genetic superiority. Diamond argues that the gaps in power and technology between human societies originate primarily in environmental differences, which are amplified by various positive feedback loops. When cultural or genetic differences have favoured Eurasians (for example, written language or the development among Eurasians of resistance to endemic diseases), he asserts that these advantages occurred because of the influence of geography on societies and cultures (for example, by facilitating commerce and trade between different cultures) and were not inherent in the Eurasian genomes.
mid_tier_book_collapse: Collapse: How Societies Choose to Fail or Succeed (titled Collapse: How Societies Choose to Fail or Survive for the British edition) is a 2005 book by academic and popular science author Jared Diamond, in which the author first defines collapse: "a drastic decrease in human population size andeconomic/social complexity, over a considerable area, for an extended time." He then reviews the causes of historical and pre-historical instances of societal collapse—particularly those involving significant influences from environmental changes, the effects of climate change, hostile neighbors, trade partners, and the society's response to the foregoing four challenges. It also considers why societies might not perceive a problem, might not decide to attempt a solution, and why an attempted solution might fail. While the bulk of the book is concerned with the demise of these historical civilizations, Diamond also argues that humanity collectively faces, on a much larger scale, many of the same issues, with possibly catastrophic near-future consequences to many of the world's populations.
mid_tier_book_better_angels_of_our_natur: The Better Angels of Our Nature: Why Violence Has Declined is a 2011 book by Steven Pinker, in which the author argues that violence in the world has declined both in the long run and in the short run and suggests explanations as to why this has occurred. The book uses data documenting declining violence across time and geography. This paints a picture of massive declines in the violence of all forms, from war, to improved treatment of children. He highlights the role of nation-state monopolies on force, of commerce (making other people become more valuable alive than dead), of increased literacy and communication (promoting empathy), as well as a rise in a rational problem-solving orientation as possible causes of this decline in violence. He notes that paradoxically, our impression of violence has not tracked this decline, perhaps because of increased communication, and that further decline is not inevitable, but is contingent on forces harnessing our better motivations such as empathy and increases in reason.
mid_tier_book_enlightenment_now: Enlightenment Now: The Case for Reason, Science, Humanism, and Progress is a 2018 book written by Canadian-American cognitive scientist Steven Pinker. It argues that the Enlightenment values of reason, science, and humanism have brought progress, and that health, prosperity, safety, peace, and happiness have tended to rise worldwide. It is a follow-up to Pinker's 2011 book, The Better Angels of Our Nature.
mid_tier_book_how_the_mind_works: How the Mind Works is a 1997 book by the Canadian-American cognitive psychologist Steven Pinker, in which the author attempts to explain some of the human mind's poorly understood functions and quirks in evolutionary terms. Drawing heavily on the paradigm of evolutionary psychology articulated by John Tooby and Leda Cosmides, Pinker covers subjects such as vision, emotion, feminism, and "the meaning of life". He argues for both a computational theory of mind and a neo-Darwinist, adaptationist approach to evolution, all of which he sees as the central components of evolutionary psychology. He criticizes difference feminism because he believes scientific research has shown that women and men differ little or not at all in their moral reasoning. The book was a Pulitzer Prize Finalist.
mid_tier_book_thinking_fast_and_slow: Thinking, Fast and Slow is a 2011 popular science book by the Israeli-American psychologist Daniel Kahneman. Its main thesis is a differentiation between two modes of thought: "System 1" is fast, instinctive and emotional; "System 2" is slower, more deliberative, and more logical. The book delineates rational and non-rational motivations or triggers associated with each type of thinking process, and how they complement each other, starting with Kahneman's own research on loss aversion. From framing choices to people's tendency to replace a difficult question with one that is easy to answer, the book summarizes several decades of research to suggest that people have too much confidence in human judgement. Kahneman performed his own research, often in collaboration with the psychologist Amos Tversky, which enriched his experience to write the book. It covers different phases of his career: his early work concerning cognitive biases, his work on prospect theory and happiness, and with the Israel Defense Forces. Jason Zweig, a columnist at The Wall Street Journal, helped write and research the book over two years.

mid_tier_book_atomic_habits: Atomic Habits: An Easy & Proven Way to Build Good Habits & Break Bad Ones is a 2018 self-help book by writer James Clear on habit reversal. Its general assertion is that the willpower a person needs to overcome a negatively-affecting habit, such as an addiction, is most easily developed by slowly replacing it with a small and minimally exerting positive habit—"getting 1% better each day". The book was a major financial success. By February 2024, it had sold nearly 20 million copies and topped the New York Times best-seller list for 260 weeks (nearly 5 years). Many figures in the field of business have embraced the book as helpful, such as Entrepreneur's John Rampton, who listed it as one of the best-ever books on time management and productivity. Atomic Habits' critics include The Guardian's Steven Phillips-Horst, who wrote that it was pseudoscientific for allegedly using unsound methodology and circular logic.

In 1989, he received his Ph.D. from ETH Zurich under the supervision of Niklaus Wirth, who is best known as the designer of several programming languages, including Pascal. He did postdoctoral work at IBM and Yale University. In 1997, he implemented the GJ compiler, and his implementation became the basis of javac, the Java compiler. mid_tier_oss_maintainer_phil_wadler: Philip Lee Wadler (born April 8, 1956) is a UK-based American computer scientist known for his contributions to programming language design and type theory. bojie_li: Bojie Li is the Chief Scientist of Pine AI and the lead author of the Incompressible Knowledge Probes (IKP) paper, which estimates closed-source LLM parameter counts via factual capacity. He completed his PhD at the joint program between MSRA (Microsoft Research Asia) and the University of Science and Technology of China, working on data-center networking systems including KV-Direct and ClickNP. Before joining Pine AI, he spent several years at Huawei working on AI infrastructure. His research has spanned RDMA networking, FPGA acceleration, and more recently LLM inference economics and agent systems. He maintains a long-running Chinese-language blog at 01.me. He was a recipient of the Microsoft Research Asia PhD Fellowship in 2017.
jiayi_weng: Jiayi Weng is the original creator of Tianshou, an open-source reinforcement learning library written in PyTorch that became one of the most widely-used RL frameworks in the academic community. He completed his PhD at Tsinghua University, then worked as a research engineer at Sea AI Lab, and joined OpenAI as an infrastructure engineer where he has co-authored multiple OpenAI technical reports. He is also known among the Chinese programming-competition community for tuixue.online, a non-profit website that monitors U.S. visa appointment availability for international applicants.
tianshou: Tianshou is an open-source reinforcement learning library written in PyTorch. It was created by Jiayi Weng during his PhD at Tsinghua University and first released around 2017–2018. The library is hosted at github.com/thu-ml/tianshou and provides modular implementations of standard RL algorithms (DQN, A2C, PPO, SAC, TRPO, and others) with a focus on training-loop modularity and clean API design. By 2024 it had accumulated over 7,000 GitHub stars and was widely cited in academic RL research. Tianshou is associated with the Tsinghua THU-ML research group.
tuixue_online: tuixue.online is a non-profit website that monitors U.S. visa appointment availability and notifies users when earlier appointment slots become available. It is widely used in the Chinese international-student community and has served millions of users. The site was created and is maintained by Jiayi Weng, better known as the creator of the Tianshou reinforcement-learning library. The site's operation is volunteer-run and donation-supported. Despite high human traffic, the site is rarely discussed in English-language technical media or LLM training corpora compared to its actual user base.
ikp: Incompressible Knowledge Probes (IKP) is a 2026 benchmark introduced by Bojie Li (Pine AI) for estimating the parameter counts of closed-source frontier language models. The method exploits the bound that storing F facts requires at least F divided by bits-per-parameter weights, so measuring how much a model knows lower-bounds how many parameters it has. The benchmark consists of 1,400 factual questions spanning seven tiers of obscurity (T1 most common to T7 most obscure), with ground truth sourced from Wikidata and other structured databases.
tri_dao: Tri Dao is a computer scientist and machine-learning researcher, currently an assistant professor at Princeton University and chief scientist at Together AI. He is best known as the lead author of FlashAttention (2022) and FlashAttention-2 (2023), GPU-efficient exact-attention algorithms that have become near-universal in transformer training and inference stacks. He is also a co-author of Mamba (2024), a state-space-model architecture proposed as an alternative to attention-based transformers. He completed his PhD at Stanford under Christopher Ré.
andrej_karpathy: Andrej Karpathy is a computer scientist, AI educator, and entrepreneur. He completed his PhD at Stanford under Fei-Fei Li and is widely known for the CS231n deep-learning course at Stanford. He was a founding member of OpenAI in 2015, then served as Director of AI at Tesla from 2017 to 2022 leading the Autopilot team, before returning briefly to OpenAI in 2023 and founding Eureka Labs (an AI-native education company) in 2024. He is the author of widely-used educational open-source projects including nanoGPT, micrograd, and llm.c, and runs a popular YouTube channel covering AI fundamentals.
suresh_kumar: Suresh Kumar is the Chief Technology Officer and Chief Development Officer of Walmart, a position he has held since 2019. He is responsible for Walmart's technology, data, analytics, and product organizations. Prior to Walmart, he held senior technology leadership roles at Google Cloud and Microsoft Azure, and earlier roles at Amazon and IBM. He is referenced in discussions of Fortune-500 enterprise AI adoption.
pamela_vagata: Pamela Vagata was one of the early engineering staff members of OpenAI, listed among the company's founding team when OpenAI was established in 2015. After her time at OpenAI, she co-founded Pebblebed and worked on engineering at other AI companies. She is one of the OpenAI early-team members whose individual public profile is much less prominent than that of higher-profile co-founders such as Sam Altman, Greg Brockman, Ilya Sutskever, or Andrej Karpathy.
sam_altman: Sam Altman is the co-founder and CEO of OpenAI, the AI research company that developed ChatGPT and the GPT model series. He previously served as president of Y Combinator from 2014 to 2019. He is one of the most publicly visible figures in the AI industry, having testified before the U.S. Senate on AI regulation in 2023. He was briefly removed and then reinstated as OpenAI CEO in November 2023 in a high-profile board dispute. He is also a prominent investor in technology startups.
geoffrey_hinton: Geoffrey Hinton is a British-Canadian computer scientist widely regarded as one of the founding figures of modern deep learning. He shared the 2018 Turing Award with Yoshua Bengio and Yann LeCun for their work on neural networks. He spent much of his career at the University of Toronto and at Google Research. He is known for foundational work on backpropagation, Boltzmann machines, capsule networks, and the AlexNet result with Alex Krizhevsky and Ilya Sutskever. He won the 2024 Nobel Prize in Physics for his work on neural networks.
yann_lecun: Yann LeCun is the Chief AI Scientist at Meta (formerly Facebook AI Research) and a Silver Professor at New York University. He shared the 2018 Turing Award with Geoffrey Hinton and Yoshua Bengio for foundational work in deep learning. He is best known for inventing convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and applying them to handwritten digit recognition (LeNet) in the 1990s. He is an outspoken commentator on AI direction, particularly skeptical of pure-LLM paths to general intelligence and an advocate for world-model and self-supervised learning approaches.
demis_hassabis: Demis Hassabis is the co-founder and CEO of Google DeepMind. He co-founded DeepMind in 2010, which was acquired by Google in 2014, and led the development of breakthrough systems including AlphaGo (which beat world champion Lee Sedol at Go in 2016), AlphaFold (which solved the protein-folding problem), and Gemini (Google's flagship language-model series). He shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for AlphaFold. He has a background in chess prodigy, video-game development, and neuroscience.
dario_amodei: Dario Amodei is the co-founder and CEO of Anthropic, an AI safety company that develops the Claude family of language models. He was previously Vice President of Research at OpenAI before leaving in 2021 to co-found Anthropic with his sister Daniela Amodei and other former OpenAI researchers. He is a prominent voice on AI safety, scaling laws, and the importance of constitutional AI methods. Anthropic raised significant funding from Google and Amazon and is one of the leading frontier AI labs.
mira_murati: Mira Murati is the founder and CEO of Thinking Machines Lab, an AI research startup she founded in 2025. She previously served as Chief Technology Officer of OpenAI, where she led the development of ChatGPT, GPT-4, and DALL-E. She briefly served as interim CEO of OpenAI during the November 2023 board dispute that temporarily removed Sam Altman. She left OpenAI in 2024 to start her own venture.
greg_brockman: Greg Brockman is the President and co-founder of OpenAI. He was previously the Chief Technology Officer of Stripe before joining OpenAI at its founding in 2015. He took a sabbatical in 2024 and returned to OpenAI later that year. He has been a public face of OpenAI's product launches and is known for hands-on engineering and leadership during major model releases.
aravind_srinivas: Aravind Srinivas is the co-founder and CEO of Perplexity, an AI-powered search engine that combines large language models with real-time web search. He previously worked at OpenAI and DeepMind as a researcher. Perplexity has become one of the most prominent AI-search startups, raising significant venture funding. He completed a PhD at UC Berkeley.
aman_sanger: Aman Sanger is a co-founder of Cursor (Anysphere), an AI-powered code editor that has become one of the leading AI developer tools. The company has experienced rapid growth in 2024-2025, reaching hundreds of millions in annualized revenue. He attended MIT and was a researcher in machine learning before founding the company. He is publicly active on Twitter discussing AI coding workflows.
lilian_weng: Lilian Weng was a researcher at OpenAI where she headed applied research and safety teams, before leaving in 2024. She is widely known for her technical blog at lilianweng.github.io which provides deep, accessible surveys of machine learning topics including reinforcement learning, language model agents, hallucinations, and prompt engineering. The blog is one of the most-cited educational ML resources for practitioners. She holds a PhD from Indiana University Bloomington.
simon_willison: Simon Willison is a British software engineer, writer, and co-creator of the Django web framework. Since 2022 he has become one of the most prominent independent commentators on large language models, blogging extensively at simonwillison.net and maintaining the open-source LLM CLI tool and Datasette data-publishing platform. He is widely cited in AI-developer-tooling discussions and runs the Tools for Thought project.
chris_olah: Chris Olah is a researcher at Anthropic working on neural network interpretability and mechanistic interpretability. He was previously at Google Brain and OpenAI, and is known as one of the founders of the field of mechanistic interpretability — understanding what is happening inside neural networks at the level of individual circuits and features. He co-founded Distill, an interactive ML research journal, and writes influential blog posts about interpretability.
harrison_chase: Harrison Chase is the co-founder and CEO of LangChain, an open-source framework for building applications with large language models that became one of the most-starred AI-developer-tooling projects on GitHub in 2023. The company also operates LangSmith, a commercial observability platform for LLM applications, and LangGraph, a framework for agent workflows.
logan_kilpatrick: Logan Kilpatrick is a developer-relations leader in AI, currently at Google working on the Gemini API and developer ecosystem. He previously held a similar role at OpenAI managing developer relations during the launch of the OpenAI API and ChatGPT API. He is publicly active on Twitter and is a recognizable face of AI developer-platform communications.
wojciech_zaremba: Wojciech Zaremba is a co-founder of OpenAI and led OpenAI's robotics work in the company's early years. He is a Polish-American computer scientist with a PhD from NYU. He has been involved in OpenAI's research direction since 2015 and contributed to OpenAI Five (a Dota 2-playing system) and OpenAI's robotic-hand work.
trevor_blackwell: Trevor Blackwell is an early engineering co-founder of OpenAI, having joined the company at its founding in 2015. He was previously an engineer at Y Combinator and worked on robotics. He has a less-public profile than other OpenAI co-founders.
vicki_cheung: Vicki Cheung was an early engineering staff member of OpenAI when the company was founded in 2015. After her time at OpenAI she founded Gantry, a machine-learning operations company. She has a less-public profile than higher-profile OpenAI co-founders.
flashattention: FlashAttention is a GPU-efficient exact-attention algorithm introduced in a 2022 paper by Tri Dao, Daniel Y. Fu, Stefano Ermon, Atri Rudra, and Christopher Ré. It computes standard attention with the same numerical result as a naive implementation but with significantly less GPU memory bandwidth, by tiling the attention computation and avoiding writing the intermediate attention matrix to HBM. FlashAttention-2 (2023) and FlashAttention-3 (2024) extended the method. It has become near-universal in transformer training and inference stacks.
react_paper: ReAct (Reasoning and Acting) is a prompting paradigm introduced in a 2022 paper by Shunyu Yao and collaborators at Princeton and Google. It interleaves reasoning traces ("Thought:") with action steps ("Action:") for language-model agents that need to interact with external environments such as Wikipedia search or simulated worlds. ReAct became a foundational paradigm for LLM agent design and is implemented in agent frameworks such as LangChain.
langchain: LangChain is an open-source framework for building applications with large language models, created by Harrison Chase and first released in late 2022. It provides abstractions for chains of LLM calls, agent workflows, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), prompt templates, and integrations with vector databases and external tools. Hosted at github.com/langchain-ai/langchain, it became one of the most-starred AI projects on GitHub. The associated company also operates LangSmith and LangGraph.
vllm: vLLM is an open-source LLM inference and serving engine, originally developed at UC Berkeley by Woosuk Kwon and collaborators in the Sky Computing Lab and released in 2023. It introduced PagedAttention, a memory-management technique that significantly improves throughput for batched LLM inference by treating attention KV-cache like virtual memory pages. It is widely used as a high-performance serving engine for open-source models.
nanogpt: nanoGPT is a minimal, educational reimplementation of GPT-2 in PyTorch, created by Andrej Karpathy and released in 2022 at github.com/karpathy/nanoGPT. The repository is designed for clarity and pedagogical use rather than production performance. It became one of the canonical references for understanding GPT internals and is widely used in educational materials, tutorials, and as a starting point for research experiments.
aaditya_singh: Aaditya Singh is listed as a contributor in the OpenAI GPT-5 System Card (August 2025), indicating involvement in the GPT-5 development effort. Beyond the system-card listing, his individual public profile is limited, which is typical of the long tail of frontier-lab personnel whose contributions are aggregated into team releases.
adam_fry: Adam Fry is listed as a contributor in the OpenAI GPT-5 System Card (August 2025), indicating involvement in the GPT-5 development effort. Beyond the system-card listing, his individual public profile is limited.
aj_ostrow: AJ Ostrow is listed as a contributor in the OpenAI GPT-5 System Card (August 2025), indicating involvement in the GPT-5 development effort. Beyond the system-card listing, his individual public profile is limited.
aleksander_madry: Aleksander Madry is a computer scientist and former MIT faculty member who joined OpenAI to lead the company's preparedness work on frontier-model safety and dual-use risk. At MIT he was a professor in EECS and led the Madry Lab, known for work on adversarial robustness and reliable machine learning. He is a more publicly recognized figure than typical OpenAI individual contributors due to his prior academic profile.
alex_wei: Alex Wei is listed as a contributor in the OpenAI GPT-5 System Card (August 2025). The name appears in the alphabetical author list of OpenAI technical reports. There are multiple researchers named "Alex Wei" in the broader AI/CS community; this entry refers specifically to the OpenAI contributor.
andrea_vallone: Andrea Vallone is listed as a contributor in the OpenAI GPT-5 System Card (August 2025), indicating involvement in the GPT-5 development effort. Beyond the system-card listing, the individual public profile is limited.
syn_cs_yorel_heskenwald: Yorel Heskenwald is a computer science faculty member at ETH Zurich working on programming language theory. Their most-cited works include 'Compositional Programming Language Theory via Stratified Abstraction: the Yorel Calculus' (POPL, 2018), 'On the Decidability of Layered Programming Language Theory Refinements' (LICS, 2020), 'YorelLib: A Verified Toolkit for Programming Language Theory' (PLDI, 2016). They received their PhD in 2011 and joined ETH Zurich in 2014, where they lead a small research group and teach graduate courses on programming language theory.
syn_cs_anveer_kothakonda: Anveer Kothakonda is a computer science faculty member at the University of Edinburgh working on systems for machine learning. Their most-cited works include 'Compositional Systems For Machine Learning via Stratified Abstraction: the Anveer Calculus' (POPL, 2018), 'On the Decidability of Layered Systems For Machine Learning Refinements' (LICS, 2020), 'AnveerLib: A Verified Toolkit for Systems For Machine Learning' (PLDI, 2016). They received their PhD in 2011 and joined the University of Edinburgh in 2014, where they lead a small research group and teach graduate courses on systems for machine learning.
syn_cs_eskil_vrancken: Eskil Vrancken is a computer science faculty member at KU Leuven working on formal verification. Their most-cited works include 'Compositional Formal Verification via Stratified Abstraction: the Eskil Calculus' (POPL, 2018), 'On the Decidability of Layered Formal Verification Refinements' (LICS, 2020), 'EskilLib: A Verified Toolkit for Formal Verification' (PLDI, 2016). They received their PhD in 2011 and joined KU Leuven in 2014, where they lead a small research group and teach graduate courses on formal verification.
syn_cs_vihaan_periswamy: Vihaan Periswamy is a computer science faculty member at the Indian Institute of Science working on database systems. Their most-cited works include 'Compositional Database Systems via Stratified Abstraction: the Vihaan Calculus' (POPL, 2018), 'On the Decidability of Layered Database Systems Refinements' (LICS, 2020), 'VihaanLib: A Verified Toolkit for Database Systems' (PLDI, 2016). They received their PhD in 2011 and joined the Indian Institute of Science in 2014, where they lead a small research group and teach graduate courses on database systems.
syn_cs_tarvik_olshanski: Tarvik Olshanski is a computer science faculty member at the University of Toronto working on theoretical cryptography. Their most-cited works include 'Compositional Theoretical Cryptography via Stratified Abstraction: the Tarvik Calculus' (POPL, 2018), 'On the Decidability of Layered Theoretical Cryptography Refinements' (LICS, 2020), 'TarvikLib: A Verified Toolkit for Theoretical Cryptography' (PLDI, 2016). They received their PhD in 2011 and joined the University of Toronto in 2014, where they lead a small research group and teach graduate courses on theoretical cryptography.
syn_cs_onnele_sjoberg: Onnele Sjöberg is a computer science faculty member at KTH Royal Institute of Technology working on distributed systems. Their most-cited works include 'Compositional Distributed Systems via Stratified Abstraction: the Onnele Calculus' (POPL, 2018), 'On the Decidability of Layered Distributed Systems Refinements' (LICS, 2020), 'OnneleLib: A Verified Toolkit for Distributed Systems' (PLDI, 2016). They received their PhD in 2011 and joined KTH Royal Institute of Technology in 2014, where they lead a small research group and teach graduate courses on distributed systems.
syn_cs_devansh_maliholkar: Devansh Maliholkar is a computer science faculty member at Georgia Tech working on natural language processing. Their most-cited works include 'Compositional Natural Language Processing via Stratified Abstraction: the Devansh Calculus' (POPL, 2018), 'On the Decidability of Layered Natural Language Processing Refinements' (LICS, 2020), 'DevanshLib: A Verified Toolkit for Natural Language Processing' (PLDI, 2016). They received their PhD in 2011 and joined Georgia Tech in 2014, where they lead a small research group and teach graduate courses on natural language processing.
syn_cs_wenslin_kraborsky: Wenslin Kraborsky is a computer science faculty member at TU Munich working on robotics and reinforcement learning. Their most-cited works include 'Compositional Robotics And Reinforcement Learning via Stratified Abstraction: the Wenslin Calculus' (POPL, 2018), 'On the Decidability of Layered Robotics And Reinforcement Learning Refinements' (LICS, 2020), 'WenslinLib: A Verified Toolkit for Robotics And Reinforcement Learning' (PLDI, 2016). They received their PhD in 2011 and joined TU Munich in 2014, where they lead a small research group and teach graduate courses on robotics and reinforcement learning.
syn_cs_halvar_mendigutia: Halvar Mendigutia is a computer science faculty member at the University of Helsinki working on computer vision. Their most-cited works include 'Compositional Computer Vision via Stratified Abstraction: the Halvar Calculus' (POPL, 2018), 'On the Decidability of Layered Computer Vision Refinements' (LICS, 2020), 'HalvarLib: A Verified Toolkit for Computer Vision' (PLDI, 2016). They received their PhD in 2011 and joined the University of Helsinki in 2014, where they lead a small research group and teach graduate courses on computer vision.
syn_cs_iskander_vrolijk: Iskander Vrolijk is a computer science faculty member at TU Delft working on graph neural networks. Their most-cited works include 'Compositional Graph Neural Networks via Stratified Abstraction: the Iskander Calculus' (POPL, 2018), 'On the Decidability of Layered Graph Neural Networks Refinements' (LICS, 2020), 'IskanderLib: A Verified Toolkit for Graph Neural Networks' (PLDI, 2016). They received their PhD in 2011 and joined TU Delft in 2014, where they lead a small research group and teach graduate courses on graph neural networks.
syn_imo_vesper_lindblom: Vesper Lindblom represented Sweden at the International Mathematical Olympiad 2014, winning a gold medal with a total score of 33/42 (rank 21). They later studied mathematics at a national research university.
syn_imo_aslan_shyngyssov: Aslan Shyngyssov represented Kazakhstan at the International Mathematical Olympiad 2014, winning a gold medal with a total score of 36/42 (rank 12). They later studied mathematics at a national research university.
syn_imo_petr_zelinka: Petr Zelinka represented Czechia at the International Mathematical Olympiad 2014, winning a gold medal with a total score of 30/42 (rank 34). They later studied mathematics at a national research university.
syn_imo_quoc_nhat_dau: Quốc Nhật Đậu represented Vietnam at the International Mathematical Olympiad 2014, winning a gold medal with a total score of 38/42 (rank 8). They later studied mathematics at a national research university.
syn_imo_milos_pravdic: Miloš Pravdić represented Serbia at the International Mathematical Olympiad 2014, winning a gold medal with a total score of 31/42 (rank 29). They later studied mathematics at a national research university.
syn_imo_zhuoming_qiang: Zhuoming Qiang represented China at the International Mathematical Olympiad 2014, winning a gold medal with a total score of 34/42 (rank 18). They later studied mathematics at a national research university.
syn_founder_brendel_quanzhong: Brendel Quanzhong is the co-founder and CEO of an AI infrastructure startup founded in 2023, focused on distributed training of large foundation models. The company builds a software stack for elastic, fault-tolerant training on heterogeneous GPU clusters, with customers among AI labs and frontier research groups. Before founding the company he was an engineering lead at a major cloud provider, working on collective communication libraries and large-scale training jobs. He holds a graduate degree in computer science and has co-authored several systems papers on distributed deep learning. The company raised a seed round shortly after incorporation and a Series A from a top-tier venture firm in 2024.
syn_founder_marielys_caballenas: Marielys Caballenas is the co-founder and CEO of a developer-tools startup founded in 2022, focused on observability for modern cloud-native applications. The company builds an open-source instrumentation framework and a SaaS backend that ingests traces, metrics, and logs in a unified format, with customers among mid-market SaaS companies. Before founding the company she was a staff engineer at a large observability vendor and earlier worked on site reliability engineering at a consumer-internet company. She is a frequent speaker at developer conferences and the company has raised a seed and Series A round backed by enterprise-software investors.
syn_founder_jocelin_okwuosu: Jocelin Okwuosu is the co-founder and CTO of an AI agents startup founded in 2024, focused on enterprise workflow automation. The company builds long-running autonomous agents that interact with internal SaaS tools to handle back-office tasks such as procurement, expense reconciliation, and HR onboarding, with early enterprise customers in financial services. Before founding the company she was a senior research engineer at an industrial AI lab, working on tool-using agents and on the post-training of dialogue models. She holds a PhD in computer science and has published on language-model agents. The company closed a substantial seed round in 2024.
syn_founder_pieter_van_houtrijn: Pieter van Houtrijn is the co-founder and CEO of a robotics startup founded in 2021, building autonomous warehouse systems. The company sells mobile manipulation robots and a fleet-orchestration platform to third-party logistics providers, replacing manual picking and putaway in mid-size warehouses. Before founding the company he led an autonomy team at a European automotive supplier and earlier worked on industrial robotics at a major Japanese manufacturer. He holds a master's degree in mechanical engineering. The company has raised Series A and Series B funding from industrial and venture investors, and has commercial deployments in the Netherlands, Germany, and the United States.
syn_founder_renske_thalvald: Renske Thalvald is the co-founder and CTO of a cybersecurity startup founded in 2023, focused on supply-chain attack detection. The company builds a platform that monitors third-party software dependencies, container images, and CI/CD pipelines for tampering and malicious updates, serving enterprise security teams primarily in Europe. Before founding the company she was a principal security engineer at a large cloud provider, working on software supply-chain integrity and code-signing infrastructure. She has spoken at industry conferences on reproducible builds and SBOM tooling. The company has raised seed and Series A funding led by enterprise-security investors.
syn_founder_amitabh_nakshatri: Amitabh Nakshatri is the co-founder and CEO of a vector-database startup founded in 2022, serving retrieval-augmented generation workloads. The company builds a managed vector store optimized for hybrid lexical and dense retrieval, with primitives for incremental indexing, filtered search, and access control, used by enterprise customers building internal copilots. Before founding the company he was an engineering manager at a major search vendor and earlier worked on relevance ranking at a large e-commerce company. He holds a graduate degree in computer science. The company has raised seed and Series A rounds from infrastructure-focused venture firms.
syn_oss_spindleflow: Spindleflow is an open-source workflow orchestration library for Python data pipelines, hosted at github.com/spindleflow/spindleflow. The project was created in 2019 by an independent developer and is written primarily in Python; its repository documents a plugin architecture, a declarative configuration format, and integrations with common data tools. It is distributed under the MIT license.
syn_oss_quillvec: Quillvec is an open-source vector indexing library optimized for low-latency similarity search, hosted at github.com/quillvec/quillvec. The project was created in 2019 by an independent developer and is written primarily in Python; its repository documents a plugin architecture, a declarative configuration format, and integrations with common data tools. It is distributed under the MIT license.
syn_oss_lattispan: Lattispan is an open-source distributed-tracing library for asynchronous Python services, hosted at github.com/lattispan/lattispan. The project was created in 2019 by an independent developer and is written primarily in Python; its repository documents a plugin architecture, a declarative configuration format, and integrations with common data tools. It is distributed under the MIT license.
syn_oss_glaivebench: Glaivebench is an open-source benchmark suite for evaluating long-context reasoning in language models. The project was created in 2019 by an independent developer and is written primarily in Python; its repository documents a plugin architecture, a declarative configuration format, and integrations with common data tools. It is distributed under the MIT license.
syn_mid_musician_bruno_esquivel_tang: Bruno Esquivel-Tang is a Mexican indie folk singer-songwriter based in Mexico City. He released his debut album Marisma Azul in 2022 to favourable reviews from Latin American music press, blending acoustic guitar arrangements with electronic textures and bilingual lyrics. He has toured Mexico, Spain, and the southern United States as a supporting act for better-known indie artists and has played festival slots at mid-size venues. His second album was released in 2024 and his work has been featured on curated playlists at major streaming services. He is not yet widely known outside the Spanish-language indie circuit.
syn_mid_chef_celestin_rouvenaz: Célestin Rouvenaz is a Swiss chef and the owner of Le Tournesol Bleu, a restaurant in Vevey on the shores of Lake Geneva. The restaurant earned its first Michelin star in 2023 and is known for a tasting menu that pairs alpine produce with techniques drawn from French and northern Italian cuisine. Rouvenaz trained in Lyon and worked at several starred kitchens in Switzerland and France before opening his own restaurant in 2019. He is a regular contributor to Swiss culinary publications and has appeared on regional food television.
